title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an extract from a travel memoir about a solo journey through Central Asia. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Road Beyond Samarkand
content.subtitle: An extract from a travel memoir about crossing Central Asia alone.
content.text: I had been warned about the road east of Samarkand. Several travellers I met in the old city had described it with a mixture of admiration and caution: long, exposed, and occasionally treacherous in ways that no map could convey. [31] One man, a Frenchman who had spent six months on a bicycle crossing the continent, told me simply: 'You either understand why people have always wanted to cross it, or you don't. There is no middle position.' [/31] I remembered his words three days later, crouched behind a crumbling stone wall, watching a dust storm advance across the plain like a slow brown wave. || The landscape between Samarkand and the Fergana Valley is not conventionally beautiful. It is vast and sparse, a terrain of clay and scrub that offers very little in the way of shelter or distraction. [32] What it does offer, in extraordinary abundance, is silence — a silence so complete that, after a few hours, I began to hear sounds I had never noticed before: the tiny percussion of grit against my jacket, the rhythm of my own breathing. [/32] I had not expected the desert to teach me how to listen. || I was travelling by a combination of shared taxis and on foot, a method that placed me in the company of an unpredictable assortment of fellow travellers. In one taxi, I shared the rear seat with a family of five, a cage of chickens, and several large bags of what turned out to be dried apricots. The mother offered me some without a word, as though sharing food with strangers required no explanation — as if hospitality were simply the default condition of life in that part of the world. [33] I accepted, and found the apricots to be the most intensely flavoured thing I had eaten in weeks. [/33] || The small town of Kokand, when I finally reached it, felt like a reward. After days of open country, its streets seemed almost luxuriously enclosed. I found a guesthouse run by a woman named Dilnoza, who spoke no English but communicated with great efficiency through gesture, expression, and occasionally through her teenage daughter, who had learned phrases from internet videos. [34] Dilnoza seemed faintly amused by my presence, in the way that experienced hosts often are — as though travellers were a recurring but harmless mystery. [/34] || It was in Kokand that I first understood what drew people to this part of the world beyond the famous Silk Road history. The answer was not ruins or ancient mosques, though these were remarkable. [35] It was the particular texture of ordinary life: the covered market at dawn, the smell of bread fresh from a clay oven, the old men playing cards in the shade of a mulberry tree. [/35] These were not performances put on for tourists. They were simply what Tuesday morning looked like in Kokand. || By the time I boarded a bus east, I had been on the road for eleven days. I was tired in a way that felt strangely comfortable — the tiredness not of illness or distress, but of full engagement with the world. [36] I thought of the Frenchman's remark again and realised, with something close to relief, that I had found my answer. [/36]

questions.question: The Frenchman's comment about the road east of Samarkand suggests that
options: A) the journey is too dangerous for inexperienced travellers.
B) people react to the route in a strongly personal way.
C) the road is more challenging on a bicycle than on foot.
D) travellers need six months to properly appreciate the landscape.
questions.explanation: The Frenchman says there is 'no middle position' — you either understand the appeal or you don't — suggesting the response to the road is deeply personal and divided.

questions.question: The writer describes the desert silence as something that
options: A) made the journey feel lonely and difficult to endure.
B) heightened their awareness of previously unnoticed details.
C) confirmed their expectation of what desert travel would feel like.
D) created a strong desire to return to a noisier environment.
questions.explanation: The complete silence caused the writer to hear 'sounds I had never noticed before', indicating that silence sharpened their perceptions rather than depressing them.

questions.question: The mother who offered the writer dried apricots is described in a way that suggests
options: A) she was trying to start a conversation despite the language barrier.
B) she expected payment for the food she gave to the writer.
C) generosity towards strangers was a natural part of her culture.
D) she was proud of the quality of the goods she was transporting.
questions.explanation: The writer describes her offering as if 'hospitality were simply the default condition of life in that part of the world', suggesting generosity was cultural rather than deliberate.

questions.question: The writer's description of Dilnoza as 'faintly amused' implies that she
options: A) found the writer's inability to speak the local language irritating.
B) regarded travellers as a familiar and slightly puzzling presence.
C) was surprised by the writer's interest in such a small town.
D) enjoyed showing off her daughter's English skills to guests.
questions.explanation: The writer compares Dilnoza's attitude to that of 'experienced hosts', for whom travellers are 'a recurring but harmless mystery' — familiar but never quite understandable.

questions.question: What does the writer say was the real appeal of the Kokand region?
options: A) The region's connections to the famous Silk Road trading routes.
B) The impressive mosques and ruins left by ancient civilisations.
C) The natural beauty of the landscape surrounding the town.
D) The unperformed, everyday activities of local people's lives.
questions.explanation: The writer states the appeal was 'the particular texture of ordinary life' and emphasises that these scenes 'were not performances put on for tourists' but genuine daily life.

questions.question: At the end of the extract, the writer feels
options: A) relieved to have finally completed such a demanding journey.
B) that the journey has confirmed a personal connection to the landscape.
C) disappointed that the trip had not lived up to expectations.
D) eager to tell others about the challenges of travelling in Central Asia.
questions.explanation: The writer recalls the Frenchman's remark about either understanding the road or not, and feels 'relief' at having found their answer — confirming a personal connection to the place.
